county_metadata.record_losses: Severe record losses occurred during the Japanese occupation of Guam (December 1941 - July 1944). Japanese forces destroyed or confiscated many civil and church records. The Battle of Guam (July-August 1944) caused further destruction during the American liberation. Many Spanish colonial-era civil records were lost; surviving colonial records are primarily Catholic Church sacramental registers and scattered documents at the Archivo General de Indias in Seville, Spain. Pre-war US Navy administration records were partially destroyed. The Guam Congress (legislature) building and government offices in Hagatna sustained heavy damage. Post-war reconstruction records begin effectively from 1944-1945. Some Japanese-period records (registration documents, occupation certificates) survive at MARC.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Guam is the largest and southernmost island in the Mariana Islands chain, located in the western Pacific Ocean. The indigenous Chamorro people have inhabited the island for approximately 4,000 years. Spanish colonization began in 1668 when Jesuit missionary Padre Diego Luis de San Vitores established the first Catholic mission. Spain administered Guam as part of the Islas Marianas (Mariana Islands) until the Spanish-American War, when the island was ceded to the United States by the Treaty of Paris on December 10, 1898. The US Navy governed Guam from 1899 to 1941, establishing civil administration, public schools, and vital records registration. Japan invaded and occupied Guam on December 10, 1941, renaming it 'Omiya Jima' (Great Shrine Island). During the occupation (1941-1944), Chamorro civilians suffered forced labor, internment, and massacres. American forces liberated Guam in the Battle of Guam (July 21 - August 10, 1944). The Organic Act of Guam (1950) established civilian self-government and granted US citizenship to Guam residents. Guam is organized into 19 villages (municipalities) for local government, but for genealogical record-keeping, the island functions as a single jurisdiction. Hagatna (formerly Agana under the US Navy and Hagadna under Spain) is the capital.
county_metadata.research_tips: Guam genealogical research centers on three overlapping record systems: (1) Catholic Church sacramental records are the single most important source for Chamorro and Spanish-era families, beginning with the first mission in 1668. The Archdiocese of Agana (now Hagatna) maintains parish registers for baptisms, marriages, burials, and confirmations -- these survived WWII better than civil records because priests hid or buried registers during the Japanese occupation. Start with the Cathedral-Basilica of Dulce Nombre de Maria (Sweet Name of Mary) in Hagatna, the oldest parish. (2) The Richard F. Taitano Micronesian Area Research Center (MARC) at the University of Guam is the premier research repository, holding Spanish colonial documents, US Navy administration records (1899-1941), Japanese occupation-era materials, oral history collections, and the Guam Recorder periodical. MARC also holds microfilm of Spanish colonial records from the Archivo General de Indias (Seville) and the Archivo Historico Nacional (Madrid). (3) The Guam Department of Public Health and Social Services, Office of Vital Statistics, holds birth and death certificates from 1901 (US Navy period) and marriage records. For Chamorro family research, understanding the Spanish surname system is essential -- Spain imposed surnames on Chamorro families in the 1850s-1860s using a catalog of approved names. The Guam Museum in Hagatna has cultural exhibits and some genealogical resources. FamilySearch has limited but growing Guam collections. For WWII-era research, NARA holds US military records of the liberation and post-war administration, including war damage claims that name families and describe property losses. The Guam War Claims Review Commission records document wartime experiences of individual families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Guam Department of Land Management maintains land title records from the US Navy period (1901); earlier Spanish-era land records are fragmentary due to WWII destruction; the Naval Government of Guam conducted land surveys and title registrations beginning in 1899; post-WWII land claims records at MARC document property restoration

source_profiles.source_name: Catholic Church Sacramental Records -- Archdiocese of Agana
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Agana (Hagatna) / Individual parish offices
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Guam, especially for the Spanish colonial period (1668-1898). The Cathedral-Basilica of Dulce Nombre de Maria in Hagatna holds the oldest registers. Sacramental records include baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), burials (entierros), and confirmations. During the Spanish period, all vital events were recorded by the Church, as there was no separate civil registration. Parish records cover all 19 villages through various parishes. Records are in Spanish (colonial era) and English/Chamorro (American era). Some parish registers have been microfilmed by FamilySearch and are held at MARC.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Uneven survival due to WWII destruction. Some parish registers survived because priests concealed them during the Japanese occupation. The earliest registers (17th-18th century) are fragmentary; 19th century registers have better survival. Post-1944 records are substantially complete.

source_profiles.repository: Guam Department of Public Health and Social Services, Office of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Birth certificates from 1901, when the US Naval Government of Guam began civil vital records registration. The Office of Vital Statistics in Mangilao maintains birth records. Pre-1901 births are documented only in Catholic Church baptismal records. Some records from 1901-1941 were damaged during the Japanese occupation. Delayed birth registrations were filed post-WWII for persons born during the occupation when no civil registration occurred.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records from 1901-1941 have gaps due to WWII destruction; Japanese occupation period (1941-1944) has minimal civil birth registration; post-1944 records are complete

source_profiles.source_name: Guam Census Records -- US Census Bureau / NARA / MARC
source_profiles.repository: NARA / US Census Bureau / Richard F. Taitano MARC, University of Guam
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The US Naval Government conducted population censuses of Guam from 1901. Guam was included in the US federal census starting in 1920 (as an outlying area). The 1930 and 1940 Guam censuses are at NARA. Earlier Spanish colonial censuses (padrones) from the 18th and 19th centuries are at the Archivo General de Indias and partially available on microfilm at MARC. The 1897 Spanish census of Guam is an important pre-American baseline. MARC holds US Navy census records and population registers from the naval government period.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Spanish colonial censuses are fragmentary; US Naval Government censuses (1901-1940) have moderate survival; some were damaged or lost during WWII; post-war censuses are complete

source_profiles.source_name: Guam Marriage Records -- Vital Statistics / Superior Court / Archdiocese
source_profiles.repository: Guam Office of Vital Statistics / Superior Court of Guam / Archdiocese of Agana
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil marriage licenses and certificates from 1901 (US Navy period). The Superior Court of Guam issues marriage licenses. Pre-1901 marriages were exclusively recorded in Catholic Church matrimonial registers (from 1668). During the Spanish period, canonical marriage was the only recognized form. The Archdiocese of Agana holds church marriage records that predate and overlap with civil registration. Marriage records during the Japanese occupation (1941-1944) are sparse.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Church marriage records have better pre-war survival than civil records; civil marriage records from 1901-1941 have gaps due to WWII losses; post-1944 civil records are complete

source_profiles.source_name: WWII and Military Records -- NARA / Guam War Claims / MARC
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Guam War Claims Review Commission / Richard F. Taitano MARC
source_profiles.coverage_notes: WWII records are critical for Guam families. NARA holds US military records of the Battle of Guam (1944), post-liberation administration records, and records of the Island Command. The Guam War Claims Review Commission records document war damage claims filed by individual families, providing names, property descriptions, and wartime experiences. Japanese occupation records include forced labor registrations, internment camp records, and civilian casualty documentation. MARC holds oral histories from survivors of the occupation. The Guam National Guard records and post-war military presence records are at NARA.
source_profiles.survival_notes: US military records at NARA are comprehensive for the liberation and post-war period; Japanese occupation-era records are scattered but some survive at MARC and Japanese national archives; war claims records are substantially complete

source_profiles.source_name: Guam Land Records -- Department of Land Management / MARC
source_profiles.repository: Guam Department of Land Management / Richard F. Taitano MARC
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land title records from the US Naval Government period (1901 forward). The Department of Land Management in Hagatna maintains current and historical land records. The Naval Government conducted cadastral surveys and title investigations in the early 1900s to establish a modern land registration system. Spanish-era land grants (mercedes de tierras) are largely lost due to WWII destruction, though some references survive in MARC collections and Archivo General de Indias. Post-WWII land claims and condemnation records (for US military bases) document family property holdings and are at MARC and NARA. Ancestral land issues remain significant in Chamorro culture.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Spanish colonial land records are mostly lost; Naval Government land records (1899-1941) suffered significant WWII damage; post-war records from land title restoration proceedings are substantially complete

source_profiles.source_name: Guam Death Records -- Vital Statistics / Cemetery Records
source_profiles.repository: Guam Office of Vital Statistics / Guam Veterans Cemetery / Parish cemeteries
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Death certificates from 1901 (US Naval Government registration). Pre-1901 deaths documented in Catholic Church burial records (entierros) from 1668. Cemetery records from parish cemeteries across the 19 villages supplement vital records. WWII civilian casualties are documented in war claims records and military after-action reports. The Guam Veterans Cemetery and various village cemeteries have burial records. The Manenggon concentration camp (1944) and other massacre sites are documented in oral histories at MARC.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Pre-WWII civil death records have gaps; Church burial records provide some coverage for the Spanish and early American periods; WWII-era deaths are partially documented through military and war claims records; post-1944 records are complete
